1: A type: “Take a sharp sword and use it as a barber’s razor — run it over your head and your beard.” The use of a sword as a razor is the deliberately violent sign: military destruction functions as a barber’s tool. The shaving of the head is a sign of extreme mourning (Job 1:20) and priestly defilement (Leviticus 21:5 — priests must not shave their heads). Ezekiel the priest is commanded to perform an act of priestly self-desecration as a prophetic sign. Christ, the ultimate High Priest, undergoes the extreme degradation of the crown of thorns (Matthew 27:29) — the thorns of the curse (Genesis 3:18) pressed into the head of the priest who bears the people’s iniquity.
2: A type: The three-part division of the hair — a third burned in the city, a third cut with a sword, a third scattered to the wind — is the judgment threefold: pestilence and famine, sword, and dispersion. The number three as a judgment structure anticipates the three-fold description of the trumpets and bowls in Revelation, where a third of creation is afflicted in each wave. Christ’s three-day death and burial is the absorbing of judgment into himself before the third-day resurrection reverses the pattern.
3: A structural note: “Take a small number of the hairs and tuck them into the folds of your cloak.” The small remnant preserved within the prophet’s cloak is the preserved remnant of Israel — a small number secured against the totality of judgment. The cloak-remnant is the OT type of the remnant preserved by grace (Romans 11:5: “at the present time there is a remnant chosen by grace”). Christ is the cloak in which the remnant is preserved — hidden in him, they are kept through the judgments that sweep away the majority.
4: A structural note: “Take some of those very hairs and throw them into the fire to burn; from that fire a flame will spread to all the house of Israel.” Even the remnant does not escape judgment entirely — fire spreads from the cloak-hairs to all Israel. The purifying fire that touches even the preserved remnant is the refiner’s fire of Malachi 3:2-3: “who can stand when he appears? For he is like a refiner’s fire... he will refine them like gold and silver.” Christ’s own death is the fire that purifies the remnant, not merely the judgment that destroys the rebellious.
5: A direct revelation: “This is Jerusalem — I placed her in the center of the nations, with countries surrounding her on all sides.” Jerusalem’s position at the center of the nations is the theological geography of the OT: the covenant city is the hub of the world’s moral order. The Christ-event is described in Galatians as occurring “in the fullness of time” (Galatians 4:4) — at the center-point of human history. Jerusalem’s geographic centrality becomes the historical centrality of the cross: the redemption accomplished at the center of the nations radiates outward to the ends of the earth (Acts 1:8: Jerusalem, Judea, Samaria, the ends of the earth).
6: A structural note: “She has turned against my laws more than the nations, and defied my statutes more than the surrounding countries.” Jerusalem’s sin is worse than the nations’ because it is covenant violation — the greater the privilege, the greater the accountability. Jesus makes the same indictment: “the men of Nineveh will rise at the judgment with this generation and condemn it, for they repented at the preaching of Jonah, and something greater than Jonah is here” (Matthew 12:41). Covenant privilege without covenant faithfulness produces the greatest condemnation.
7: A structural note: “Because you have exceeded the nations around you in rebellion.” The comparative judgment — not merely sinning but exceeding the surrounding nations in sin — echoes the logic of Amos 1-2, where Israel’s judgment is the climax of judgments on the surrounding nations. Jesus’s judgment on Capernaum is similarly comparative: “if the mighty works done in you had been done in Sodom, it would have remained until this day. But I tell you that it will be more tolerable on the day of judgment for the land of Sodom than for you” (Matthew 11:23-24).
8: A direct revelation: “I — yes, I myself — am against you, and I will carry out my judgments against you in the full sight of the nations.” The repeated first-person emphasis (gam ani, I myself) makes YHWH the judge of his own covenant people in the presence of the nations. The cross is the ultimate judgment carried out in full sight: Christ, bearing the judgment of the covenant people, dies publicly, before the assembled representatives of the nations (Jews from every nation, Acts 2:5; Roman soldiers; Gentile authorities). The judgment in full sight of the nations is fulfilled in the crucifixion.
9: A structural note: “I will do to you what I have never done before, and what I will never do again, on account of all your abominations.” The unprecedented quality of YHWH’s judgment on Jerusalem has a corresponding unprecedented quality in Christ’s atonement: the cross is the once-for-all sacrifice (Hebrews 9:26: “he has appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself”). The unique, unrepeatable character of the judgment on Jerusalem finds its redemptive counterpart in the unique, unrepeatable sacrifice of the Son of God.
10: A structural note: “Fathers will eat their sons within you, and sons will eat their fathers.” The cannibalism of extreme famine — the ultimate inversion of family and human order — is the covenant curse of Leviticus 26:29 and Deuteronomy 28:53-57, historically fulfilled in the sieges of 586 BCE (Lamentations 4:10) and 70 CE (Josephus, Jewish War). Christ weeps over the coming horror (Luke 19:41-44) rather than inflicting it with detachment — the judge of Jerusalem is also its mourner.
11: A direct revelation: “Because you defiled my sanctuary with all your vile practices and abominations, I will cut you down; my eye will not spare and I will not show compassion.” The defilement of the sanctuary triggers YHWH’s full judicial withdrawal of mercy. The temple cleansings in the NT (John 2:13-22; Matthew 21:12-13) are Jesus’s prophetic declaration that the same defilement is happening in the Second Temple. Jesus’s first cleansing citation — “my house will be called a house of prayer, but you are making it a den of robbers” — is precisely Ezekiel’s complaint in priestly-judicial form.
12: A type: “A third of you will die by plague and be consumed by famine within the city; a third will fall by the sword around you; and a third I will scatter to every wind.” The threefold judgment — plague/famine, sword, scattering — is the complete dismantling of the covenant community. Christ’s death absorbs all three modes of covenant judgment: he experiences plague-like desolation (“my God, why have you forsaken me,” citing Ps 22’s plague imagery), dies by the sword of Roman execution, and his disciples scatter (“you will all fall away,” Mark 14:27, citing Zechariah 13:7). The shepherd absorbs what would otherwise scatter the flock.
13: A structural note: “Then my anger will be exhausted, my fury will rest upon them and find its end... when I have spent my fury on them.” The language of divine fury being “spent” and “finding its end” (venachamti, and I will be comforted/satisfied) is the OT theology of propitiation: YHWH’s anger burns until it has fully accomplished judgment and is then satisfied. The cross is where YHWH’s fury over sin is fully spent on Christ: the propitiation (Romans 3:25; 1 John 2:2) means that the divine fury finds its end in the person of the Son rather than in the destruction of his people.
14: A structural note: “I will make you a ruin and a disgrace among the surrounding nations, in the sight of all who pass by.” Jerusalem as a ruin passed by with horror — the covenant city becoming a warning to the nations — is the Lamentations motif (Lamentations 1:12: “is it nothing to you, all you who pass by?”). The same phrasing is applied by the mockers at the cross: “those who passed by derided him, wagging their heads” (Mark 15:29). Christ on the cross is the covenant city in miniature — the one who embodies Israel bearing her judgment, becoming a spectacle before passing nations.
15: A structural note: “You will be a disgrace and a mockery, a warning and a horror, to the surrounding nations when I carry out my judgments against you in anger.” The language of “disgrace, mockery, warning, horror” precisely describes Christ on the cross from the watching crowd’s perspective: “he saved others; let him save himself, if he is the Christ of God” (Luke 23:35); “if you are the King of the Jews, save yourself” (Luke 23:37). The watching nations saw only the disgrace; the cross as warning and horror was transparent only to faith.
16: A structural note: “When I launch against you the deadly arrows of famine aimed at destruction.” The arrows of famine as divine weapons echo the bow-and-arrow imagery of divine judgment in Psalms 38:2 and Lamentations 3:12-13. Christ at Gethsemane asks if the cup can pass from him (Matthew 26:39) — the cup of divine judgment including famine, sword, plague. Having absorbed the arrows of divine wrath, he becomes the source of bread for the world: “I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger” (John 6:35).
17: A structural note: “I will unleash famine, ferocious beasts, pestilence, bloodshed, and the sword upon you. I, the LORD, have spoken.” The five-fold judgment (famine, beasts, pestilence, bloodshed, sword) echoes Ezekiel 14:21’s four sore judgments and anticipates Rev 6’s four horsemen. Christ’s work reverses each: he provides bread (famine), is “the Lion of the tribe of Judah” (beast reversed), heals diseases (pestilence), shed no blood unjustly (bloodshed), and was himself pierced by the sword (John 19:34; Zechariah 13:7). The five-fold judgment absorbed by Christ becomes the ground of the five-fold blessing of the new covenant.
